text: This is a video demonstration of laparoscopic colonic pull-through and delayed colloanal anastomosis for colonic necrosis after robotic intersphincter resection. Our patient is a sixty-three year old man with long-standing hypertension and diabetes and a smoker for forty years. He is on polypharmacy and has moderate coronary and carotid calcifications. He also gives history of syncopal attacks and has passed pulmonary tuberculosis with preserved pulmonary functions. He presented to us with a rectal cancer five centimeters from the anal verge that was moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma and had no distant metastasis. On the baseline MRI, he had a T-III lesion with mesorectal nodes. The mesorectal fascia was uninvolved, the sphincter complex was free and there were no lateral pelvic nodes.

He received long-course chemoradiation with brachytherapy boost and there was partial response on the MRI. He underwent a robotic intersphincter resection with a diverting ileostomy. He was discharged unwindfully on post-operative day six. The final histology showed a YPT-N-Zero disease with a tumor regression grade of two by five. However, he was readmitted on day thirteen with fevers and a drain site abscess. The rectal examination demonstrated a complete anastomotic disruption with purulent discharge.

The CT scan reveals complete anastomotic dehiscence with air in the pelvis and abdomen. During emergency exploration, pus is observed in the anal canal, and the visible colon is sloughed. Laparoscopic inspection shows pus and slough in the pelvis with dense inflammatory adhesions between the bladder and colonic conduit. The surgeon carefully releases these adhesions using suction and continuous irrigation, avoiding bladder injury by staying close to the pericolic fat and sigmoid wall.

The surgeon completes the pelvic dissection and focuses on separating the mesentery of the left colon from the retroperitoneum. The dissection proceeds smoothly in the correct retroperitoneal plane, avoiding injury to retroperitoneal structures. The retroperitoneal and pelvic dissections are connected, allowing the ischemic colon to be delivered into the abdomen for examination. The line of demarcation is clearly visible. The pelvic cavity is thoroughly irrigated, and additional mobilization of the splenic flexure is needed to deliver the colonic conduit from the anal canal. The small bowel is gently dissected off from the inferior mesenteric artery clips and the retroperitoneum using constant irrigation.

The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure from the inframesocolic and suprapancreatic sides, performing retroperitoneal dissection cranially over the pancreas. The lesser sac is entered, and the root of the transverse mesocolon is divided. The splenic flexure is then approached laterally by dividing the omental attachments to the left and transverse colon, reentering the lesser sac from below, allowing for complete mobilization of the splenic flexure and transverse colon.

The surgeon delivers the colon into the pelvis and out of the anal canal, ensuring proper orientation to prevent twisting of the mesentery. The small bowel is positioned over the colonic mesentery to prevent internal herniation. The pelvis is thoroughly irrigated, and the bladder is inspected for potential leaks following the repeat mobilization.

The colon is left prolapsing three centimeters out of the anal canal, secured with cardinal stitches at four places. The final image shows the revised colonic conduit with good anal tone after a redo coloanal anastomosis performed under regional anesthesia.

The patient is yet to undergo a reversal of the stoma. Despite the use of ICG in the primary operation, a long segment colonic necrosis was found. We hypothesize that this is likely due to delayed ischemia from suboptimal perfusion rather than absent perfusion. Since ICG was assessed subjectively by the visual appearance of fluorescence rather than objective perfusion parameters, a de novo ischemia is a distinct possibility. Splenic flexure mobilization in the index operation is performed from the lateral side on an as-needed basis for attention-free anastomosis.

During the repeat operation, the surgeon mobilizes the colon from the medial side over the pancreas body, performing gastrocolic omental division to achieve greater length without entering the previous operative field. This laparoscopic approach is demonstrated amidst acute inflammation.
